protocols.notes_updates: Definition of Acute Liver Failure (ALF): Rapid development of severe acute liver injury characterised by impaired synthetic function (INR >1.5) and complicated by development of encephalopathy, in a person without history of liver disease or who had well-compensated liver disease | Causes (DAVES mnemonic): Drugs (paracetamol, TB drugs, ARVs, NSAIDs, traditional medicine, illicit drugs), Alcohol, Viral (Hep A/B/C/E, EBV, CMV, HSV, HZV, ParvoB19), Extras (acute fatty liver of pregnancy, HELLP, ischaemic necrosis, vascular, metabolic, autoimmune, Wilson's disease, Budd-Chiari, idiopathic, heatstroke, Reye's, malignancy), Sepsis | WEST HAVEN GRADING OF HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY: Grade 0 (minimal/subclinical): No clinical manifestations, some abnormalities on psychometric testing | Grade I (mild): Euphoria/depression, mild confusion, slurred speech, disordered sleep, asterixis may/may not be present | Grade II (moderate): Lethargy, moderate confusion, asterixis usually present | Grade III (severe): Marked confusion, incoherent speech, drowsy but rousable, asterixis present | Grade IV (coma): Comatose | Alternate HE Grading - Intellectual and Neuromuscular Function: Grade I: Impaired attention/irritable/depressed/personality changes; Tremor/apraxia/incoordination | Grade II: Drowsy/poor memory/behavioural changes/sleep disturbances; Asterixis/ataxia/slow slurred speech | Grade III: Confusion/somnolence/disorientation/amnesia; Hypoactive reflexes/clonus/nystagmus/muscle rigidity | Grade IV: Stupor/coma; Dilated pupils/decerebrate posture/oculocephalic reflex absent | COMPLICATIONS OF ALF: Neurological (hepatic encephalopathy, cerebral oedema, seizures, raised ICP), Coagulopathy and bleeding, Metabolic (hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia, lactic acidosis, hyperammonaemia), Infection (septicaemia, peritonitis, pneumonia, UTI), Respiratory (hypoxia, aspiration, ARDS), Renal (hepatorenal syndrome, ATN), Cardiovascular (hypotension, arrhythmias) | MANAGEMENT: ABCD approach | Airway and Breathing: Intubate if grade III-IV encephalopathy | Ventilate to normocapnia | Circulation: Fluid resuscitation | Vasopressors (target MAP >65mmHg) | Invasive monitoring | Monitor for and treat sepsis | Neurological: Grade I-II: Lactulose 30ml PO TDS, Rifaximin 550mg PO BD | Grade III-IV: Lactulose via NGT (titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day) | Mannitol 0.5-1g/kg for cerebral oedema | Monitor ICP if available | Consider thiopentone coma for refractory raised ICP | Metabolic: Frequent glucose monitoring (hypoglycaemia common) | 50% Dextrose if glucose <4mmol/L | Electrolyte replacement (K+, PO4, Mg2+) | Coagulopathy: Vitamin K 10mg IV (unless alcoholic) | FFP if active bleeding or invasive procedure | Platelets if <10 or <50 with bleeding | Cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1g/L | Infection surveillance: Daily cultures | Empirical antibiotics if suspected sepsis | Hepatorenal syndrome: Terlipressin 0.5-2mg IV QDS + Albumin 1g/kg/day | N-acetylcysteine if paracetamol toxicity or unknown cause | Consider transplant referral early (King's College criteria)

drugs.notes: If paracetamol toxicity or unknown cause
